text: The surgical footage shows the dissection of the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures during hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the hernia sac and esophagus, ensuring identification and preservation of the vagal nerves. The esophagus is dissected to achieve at least three centimeters of distal esophageal length within the abdomen, using a high mediastinal dissection technique to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening, or hiatus, with sutures to achieve a tension-free repair. A mesh is then placed over the incision to reinforce the closure, reducing the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.

The esophagus and stomach are visible, prepared for surgical manipulation. A rubber band encircles the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), with the esophagus positioned above and the stomach below. A white mesh is visible at the top, indicating the site of a previous hiatal hernia repair. The surgeon pushes the top part of the stomach behind and around the esophagus, moving it back and forth to ensure it is not too tight, which could restrict blood flow. The stomach wrap is then sutured, with the first suture connecting stomach tissue to stomach tissue, the second suture passing through the esophagus, and the third suture through the LES.
The surgeon completes the Nissen fundoplication, finalizing the 360-degree wrap of the stomach around the lower esophageal sphincter to enhance reflux control.
